Constraints in MySQL are rules applied to table columns to ensure the accuracy, validity, and integrity of the data.
Ensures that all values in a column are different.

UNIQUE

ALTER TABLE

ALTER TABLE users
ADD CONSTRAINT unique_email UNIQUE (email);
Ensures that a column cannot contain NULL values.
Change an existing column to NOT NULL:

ALTER TABLE users
MODIFY COLUMN name VARCHAR(100) NOT NULL;

ALTER TABLE users
MODIFY COLUMN name VARCHAR(100) NULL;
Ensures that values in a column satisfy a specific condition.
Example: Allow only dates of birth after Jan 1, 2000

ALTER TABLE users
ADD CONSTRAINT chk_dob CHECK (date_of_birth > '2000-01-01');
Naming the constraint () helps if you want to drop it later.
Sets a default value for a column if none is provided during insert.
Uniquely identifies each row. Must be NOT NULL and UNIQUE.
Used withto automatically assign the next number.
Each new row gets the next available integer value in.

By default, MySQL operates in AutoCommit mode. This means that every SQL
statement is treated as a transaction and is committed automatically. However, for more control over when changes are saved, you can turn AutoCommit off and
When AutoCommit is off, you can explicitly control when to commit or rollback changes.
This turns off AutoCommit, meaning that changes you make won’t be saved to the database unless you explicitly tell MySQL to commit them.

COMMITImportant: Until you execute a, your changes are not permanent.
COMMIT
COMMIT — Save Changes to the Database
Once you’ve made changes and you’re confident that everything is correct, you can

COMMITuse thecommand to save those changes.
COMMIT

COMMIT;
COMMITROLLBACKThis saves all the changes made since the last point, the changes become permanent.
COMMIT
ROLLBACK
ROLLBACK — Revert Changes to the Last Safe Point
If you make an error or decide you don’t want to save your changes, you can
rollback the transaction to its previous state.
This undoes all changes since the lastor.
Here’s a simple example of using

COMMITand
COMMIT
ROLLBACKin a transaction:
ROLLBACK
Make some changes (e.g., updating a salary):
UPDATE users SET salary = 80000 WHERE id = 5;
Decide whether to commit or rollback:
If you’re happy with the changes, run:
If you’re not happy and want to revert the changes, run:
If you want to turn AutoCommit back on (so that every statement is automatically committed), you can do so with:
when you want to make changes permanent.

COMMITROLLBACKto discard changes if something goes wrong.

ROLLBACK
Consider disabling AutoCommit when performing complex updates to avoid saving partial or incorrect data.
Ais a constraint in SQL that uniquely identifies each row in a table.

PRIMARY KEYIt is one of the most important concepts in database design.
PRIMARY KEY
Is used to identify rows in a table
Can be a single column or a combination of columns
Each table can have only one primary key

A foreign key is a column that creates a link between two tables. It ensures that the value in one table must match a value in another table.
This is used to maintain data integrity between related data.

usersYou have atable. Now you want to store each user’s address. Instead of
users

addressesuserstable, and link it to
addresses
users
table, you create a separate using a foreign key.
addressesCreating a Table with a Foreign Key
Let’s create antable where each address belongs to a user.

CREATE TABLE addresses (
id INT AUTO_INCREMENT PRIMARY KEY,
user_id INT,
street VARCHAR(255), city VARCHAR(100),
state VARCHAR(100),
pincode VARCHAR(10),
FOREIGN KEY (user_id) REFERENCES users(id)
);

user_idis a foreign key.
user_id
idIt references the
id

usersThis ensures that every address must be linked to a valid user.
users
To drop a foreign key, you need to know its constraint name. MySQL auto-generates it if you don’t specify one, or you can name it yourself:

ALTER TABLE addresses
DROP FOREIGN KEY fk_user;
Adding a Foreign Key Later (Using ALTER)
Suppose the foreign key was not defined during table creation. You can add it
By default, if you delete a user that has related addresses, MySQL will throw an

ON DELETEerror. You can control this behavior with.
ON DELETE
If you want addresses to be automatically deleted when the user is deleted:

CREATE TABLE addresses (
id INT AUTO_INCREMENT PRIMARY KEY,
user_id INT,
street VARCHAR(255), city VARCHAR(100),
state VARCHAR(100),
pincode VARCHAR(10),
CONSTRAINT fk_user FOREIGN KEY (user_id) REFERENCES users(id) ON DELETE CASCADE
);

ALTER TABLE addresses
ADD CONSTRAINT fk_user FOREIGN KEY (user_id) REFERENCES users(id) ON DELETE CASCADE;
Deletes all related rows in child table
Sets the foreign key to NULL in the child table
Prevents deletion of parent if child exists (default)
Foreign keys connect tables and enforce valid references.

You can drop them by name.

A view in MySQL is a virtual table based on the result of aquery. It does

SELECTnot store data itself — it always reflects the current data in the base tables. Views are useful when:
SELECT
You want to simplify complex queries
You want to hide certain columns from users
You want a “live snapshot” of filtered data
Suppose we want a view that lists all users earning more than ₹70,000.
This will return all users from thetable where salary is above ₹70,000.
Demonstrating That a View is Always Up-To-Date
Let’s see what happens when the underlying data changes.

UPDATE users
SET salary = 72000 WHERE name = 'Raj';
Notice how Raj is now included in the view — without updating the view
itself. That’s because views always reflect live data from the original table.
Views act like saved SELECT queries
Changes to base tables are reflected automatically
Great for simplifying complex queries or creating filtered access
Indexes in MySQL are used to speed up data retrieval. They work like the index of a book — helping the database engine find rows faster, especially for searches, filters, and joins.

A subquery is a query nested inside another query. Subqueries are useful for breaking down complex problems into smaller parts.
Suppose we want to find all users who earn more than the average salary of all users.
This subquery returns a single value — the average salary — and we compare each user’s salary against it.

SELECT AVG(salary) FROM usersreturns the average salary.

SELECT AVG(salary) FROM users
The outer query selects all users with a salary greater than that average.
Now let’s say we want to find users who have been referred by someone who earns more than ₹75,000.

SELECT id FROM users WHERE salary > 75000The inner query:

SELECT id FROM users WHERE salary > 75000

WHERE referred_by_id IS NOT NULL GROUP BY referred_by_id;

To get subtotals and grand totals, you can use:

SELECT gender, COUNT(*) AS total_users FROM users
GROUP BY gender WITH ROLLUP;
This will give you a count of users by gender, along with a grand total for all users.

A stored procedure is a saved SQL block that can be executed later. It’s useful when you want to encapsulate logic that can be reused multiple times — like queries, updates, or conditional operations.
By default, MySQL usesto end SQL statements.
can confuse MySQL. To avoid this, we temporarily change the delimiter (e.g. to
Syntax for Creating a Stored Procedure
DELIMITER $$CREATE PROCEDURE procedure_name()BEGIN-- SQL statements go here END$$DELIMITER ;

DELIMITER $$
CREATE PROCEDURE procedure_name()
BEGIN
-- SQL statements go here END$$
DELIMITER ;
After the procedure is created, we reset the delimiter back to.
usersCreating a Procedure with Input Parameters
Let’s say you want to create a stored procedure that inserts a new user into the table.

DELIMITER $$CREATE PROCEDURE AddUser(IN p_name VARCHAR(100),IN p_email VARCHAR(100),IN p_gender ENUM('Male', 'Female', 'Other'), IN p_dob DATE,IN p_salary INT)BEGININSERT INTO users (name, email, gender, date_of_birth, salary) VALUES (p_name, p_email, p_gender, p_dob, p_salary);END$$DELIMITER ;

DELIMITER $$
CREATE PROCEDURE AddUser(
IN p_name VARCHAR(100),
IN p_email VARCHAR(100),
IN p_gender ENUM('Male', 'Female', 'Other'), IN p_dob DATE,
IN p_salary INT
)
BEGIN
INSERT INTO users (name, email, gender, date_of_birth, salary) VALUES (p_name, p_email, p_gender, p_dob, p_salary);
END$$
DELIMITER ;
This creates a procedure namedthat accepts five input parameters.
You can call the procedure using:
This will insert the new user into thetable.
Input parameters are declared using thekeyword.

INStored procedures are stored in the database and can be reused.
